1:
"Now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house, unto a land that I will shew thee:" The witnesses note the call of Abram to leave his country for the land God would show him. On "the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country," the grammatical voices note the call. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country" — God called Abram to leave his country (the call given first in Ur, Acts 7:2-4, and renewed at Haran); the pluperfect "had said" connects the call to Abram's departure from Ur (11:31); the divine summons to forsake all and follow God to an unknown land.

On "thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the threefold renunciation. Henry and Calvin note the threefold leaving — "thy country... thy kindred... thy father's house" — Abram must forsake his native land, his wider kin, and his own family; the call demanded a complete separation from all that was dear and familiar; the test of Abram's faith and obedience, to leave all at God's word.

On "unto a land that I will shew thee," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the faith. Clarke and Keil note that Abram was called "unto a land that I will shew thee" — an unnamed, unknown land; he went out, not knowing whither he went (Hebrews 11:8); the call required faith — to leave the known for the unknown, on the bare word of God; the obedience of faith, trusting God's promise and guidance; the pattern of the believer's pilgrimage. The verse displays the call of Abram to leave his country.

The witnesses agree: the call — "the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country," God calling Abram to leave his land (Acts 7:2-4, Keil, Ellicott); "thy country... thy kindred... thy father's house," the threefold renunciation, a complete separation (Henry, Calvin); "unto a land that I will shew thee," the call to an unknown land, the obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:8, Clarke, Keil). The verse displays the called Abram. The verse marks the call: get thee out. The verse displays the renunciation: country, kindred, house. The verse displays the land: unknown. The verse displays the faith: required. The call displays God summoning Abram to forsake all and follow. The verse displays the Abram: called. The verse displays the leaving: complete. The verse displays the faith: tested. The call of Abram to leave his country displays the summons of faith: the LORD said, Get thee out of thy country, kindred, and father's house, unto a land I will show thee — the call to forsake all and follow God to an unknown land, the obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:8). The verse displays the called Abram, with the great promise (vv.2-3).
2:
"And I will make of thee a great nation, and I will bless thee, and make thy name great; and thou shalt be a blessing:" The witnesses note the promise of a great nation, blessing, and a great name. On "I will make of thee a great nation," the grammatical voices note the nation. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I will make of thee a great nation" — God promises Abram a numerous posterity, a great nation (Israel, and the multitude of his spiritual seed); the promise of a great nation to a man whose wife was barren (11:30); the human impossibility against which God's power would be displayed.

On "I will bless thee, and make thy name great," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the blessing and name. Calvin and Gill note that God would "bless thee, and make thy name great" — God's favor and blessing on Abram, and the exalting of his name; in striking contrast to the builders of Babel, who sought to "make us a name" (11:4) by their own pride — God himself would make Abram's name great; Abram's name, honored by God, would be revered through all generations (as it is among Jews, Christians, and Muslims).

On "thou shalt be a blessing," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the channel of blessing: "thou shalt be a blessing" — Abram would not only be blessed but be a blessing, a fountain of blessing to others; through him and his seed, blessing would flow to the nations (v.3); the called man made a means of grace to the world; the blessing of Abram to overflow to all. The verse displays the promise of a great nation, blessing, and a great name.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the nation — "I will make of thee a great nation," the promise of a numerous posterity to a barren man (11:30, Keil, Gill); "I will bless thee, and make thy name great," God's blessing and the exalting of Abram's name, in contrast to Babel (11:4, Calvin, Gill); "thou shalt be a blessing," Abram a fountain of blessing to the nations (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised blessing. The verse marks the nation: great. The verse displays the blessing: God's. The verse displays the name: made great. The verse displays the Abram: a blessing. The promise displays God blessing Abram and making him a blessing. The verse displays the nation: promised. The verse displays the name: exalted. The verse displays the blessing: overflowing. The promise of a great nation, blessing, and a great name displays God's covenant favor: I will make of thee a great nation, bless thee, make thy name great, and thou shalt be a blessing — the promise of posterity, favor, and an honored name (against Babel's self-made name, 11:4), Abram made a fountain of blessing to the nations. The verse displays the blessed Abram, the blessing of all families (v.3).
3:
"And I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee: and in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed." The witnesses note the promise of blessing to all families of the earth in Abram — the crux of the Gospel promise. On "I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee," the grammatical voices note God's identification with Abram. Gill and Calvin note that "I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee" — God so identifies himself with Abram (and his seed) that he would bless those who blessed him and curse those who cursed him; God's protecting favor over Abram and his line; the friends of Abram's seed God's friends, the enemies God's enemies; the special providence over the chosen line.

On "in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed," interpreters expound the great promise. The reformed Calvin and the dominant evangelical reading take it as the Gospel promise of blessing to all nations in Abram's seed, Christ: "in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed" — all the families/nations of the earth would be blessed in Abram, that is, in his seed, the Messiah, who would spring from him (Galatians 3:8, "the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham"); the promise is to Abram's seed, "which is Christ" (Galatians 3:16); in Christ, of Abram's line, the Gospel would be preached to all nations, and all who believe (of every nation) would be blessed with justification and salvation; the universal blessing of the Gospel, the heart of the Abrahamic covenant. A secondary aspect notes that Abram and his posterity (the true religion, the Scriptures, the line of the church) were a channel of blessing to the world.

On the universal blessing, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the scope: "all families of the earth" — men of all nations and every age, Jew and Gentile, would be blessed in Abram's seed; the breadth of the promise embraces the whole human race; the call of Abram, far from narrowing God's mercy to one people, was the means of blessing to all; the Gospel, preached throughout the world, fulfils the promise. The verse displays the promise of blessing to all families in Abram.

The witnesses agree the substance: God would bless Abram's friends and curse his foes, and in Abram all families of the earth would be blessed (Gill, Calvin, Clarke). The promise "in thee shall all families be blessed" bears its great sense as a crux: all nations blessed in Abram's seed, the Messiah, Christ — the Gospel promise of justification and salvation to all who believe (the dominant evangelical reading, Galatians 3:8, 16) vs a more general blessing through Abram and his posterity (the channel of the true religion). The verse displays the Gospel-promise crux. The verse marks the blessing: of all families. The verse displays the debate: in Christ the seed, or through Abram generally. The verse displays the nations: all blessed. The verse displays the Gospel: preached to Abraham. The promise displays the universal blessing in Abram's seed. The verse displays the families: blessed. The verse displays the seed: Christ. The verse displays the Gospel: foreshown. The promise of blessing to all families in Abram displays the Gospel and a crux: in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed — read by the dominant evangelical view as the blessing of justification and salvation to all nations in Abram's seed, Christ (the Gospel preached to Abraham, Galatians 3:8, 16), and more generally as the blessing flowing through Abram and his posterity. The verse displays the universal promise, obeyed by Abram (v.4).
4:
"So Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him; and Lot went with him: and Abram was seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran." The witnesses note Abram's obedient departure from Haran. On "So Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him," the grammatical voices note the obedience. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him" — Abram obeyed the call, departing from Haran as God had commanded; the prompt, unquestioning obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:8); he went out, trusting God's word, to the land God would show him; the obedience that followed the call (vv.1-3).

On "and Lot went with him," the puritan Henry and witnesses note Lot. Henry and Gill note that "Lot went with him" — Abram's nephew Lot accompanied him; the family of the righteous traveling together; Lot, who would share in Abram's journeyings (and later separate, ch. 13); the companion of Abram's pilgrimage.

On "Abram was seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the age. Clarke and Ellicott note that Abram was seventy-five when he left Haran — an advanced age to begin a pilgrimage to an unknown land; the obedience of faith at a time of life when men seek rest; Abram, old and childless, set out on God's promise; the dating of the departure marks the beginning of the patriarchal history. The verse displays Abram's obedient departure from Haran.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him," Abram's prompt obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:8, Ellicott, Gill); "and Lot went with him," Lot accompanying Abram (Henry, Gill); "seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran," Abram's advanced age at the start of his pilgrimage (Clarke, Ellicott). The verse displays the departing Abram. The verse marks the departure: obedient. The verse displays the Lot: with him. The verse displays the age: 75. The verse displays the faith: shown. The obedience displays Abram departing at God's word. The verse displays the Abram: departing. The verse displays the Lot: accompanying. The verse displays the pilgrimage: begun. Abram's obedient departure from Haran displays the obedience of faith: Abram departed as the LORD had spoken, Lot with him, at seventy-five years — the prompt obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:8), the old and childless man setting out on God's promise for an unknown land. The verse displays the departing Abram, entering Canaan (v.5).
5:
"And Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son, and all their substance that they had gathered, and the souls that they had gotten in Haran; and they went forth to go into the land of Canaan; and into the land of Canaan they came." The witnesses note Abram's journey with his household into Canaan. On "Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son," the grammatical voices note the company. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son" — Abram set out with his wife Sarai and his nephew Lot; the household of the patriarch; the company of the pilgrimage to Canaan.

On "all their substance... and the souls that they had gotten in Haran," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the substance and souls. Calvin and Gill note that they took "all their substance (their possessions, flocks, goods) and the souls that they had gotten in Haran" — their accumulated wealth, and the "souls" (the servants, dependents, and perhaps proselytes — those gained or won, some say converted to the true religion, in Haran); the considerable household of Abram, with its possessions and persons; some take "the souls they had gotten" as those won to the faith.

On "into the land of Canaan they came," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the arrival: "into the land of Canaan they came" — Abram reached the land God had promised to show him (v.1); the pilgrimage's first goal attained; Canaan, the promised land, entered; the patriarch arrives in the land of promise, to sojourn there as a stranger (Hebrews 11:9). The verse displays Abram's journey with his household into Canaan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the company — "Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son," the household setting out (Keil, Gill); "all their substance... and the souls that they had gotten in Haran," their possessions and dependents (some, proselytes, Calvin, Gill); "into the land of Canaan they came," Abram reaching the promised land (Hebrews 11:9, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying household. The verse marks the company: Sarai and Lot. The verse displays the substance: gathered. The verse displays the souls: gotten. The verse displays the Canaan: reached. The journey displays Abram's household entering the land of promise. The verse displays the Abram: journeying. The verse displays the household: with him. The verse displays the Canaan: entered. Abram's journey with his household into Canaan displays the pilgrimage to the promised land: Abram took Sarai, Lot, their substance, and the souls gotten in Haran, and came into Canaan — the patriarch reaching the land God promised to show him (v.1), to sojourn there as a stranger (Hebrews 11:9). The verse displays the arrived Abram, at Shechem (v.6).
6:
"And Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem, unto the plain of Moreh. And the Canaanite was then in the land." The witnesses note Abram's passing to Shechem and the note that the Canaanite was then in the land — the crux of authorship. On "Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem," the grammatical voices note the place. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem (Shechem), unto the plain (oak/terebinth) of Moreh" — Abram traveled through Canaan to Shechem, a central place in the land; the oak/plain of Moreh, a notable spot; the patriarch journeying through the land that was promised to his seed.

On "the Canaanite was then in the land," interpreters divide over the phrase and its bearing on authorship. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and some hold the phrase is a later note or interpolation: "the Canaanite was then in the land" seems to be written from the standpoint of a time when the Canaanites were no longer in the land (after their expulsion by Israel); on this view the note is a later editorial gloss (or evidence of post-Mosaic composition), explaining that, in Abram's day, the land was still held by the Canaanites. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and the conservative reading hold it is no sign of post-Mosaic authorship nor an interpolation: the note simply states that, when Abram came, the Canaanites already occupied the land (so that Abram was a stranger in a land not yet his, possessed by others); Moses, the author, could write it as a plain historical observation (the Canaanites being still in the land in his own day, before the conquest); the phrase requires no later hand.

On the note, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the point: whatever its bearing on authorship, the note marks that Abram, to whom the land was promised, found it already inhabited by the Canaanites; he was a stranger and sojourner in the land of promise (Hebrews 11:9), possessing none of it, walking by faith in God's promise to give it to his seed (v.7). The verse displays Abram's passing to Shechem and the note of the Canaanite.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abram passed to Shechem, and the Canaanites then held the land (Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The note "the Canaanite was then in the land" bears a crux: a later editorial gloss or evidence of post-Mosaic composition (written when the Canaanites were gone — Clarke) vs no sign of post-Mosaic authorship, a plain note by Moses that the land was already occupied in Abram's day (Ellicott). The verse displays the authorship crux. The verse marks the place: Shechem. The verse displays the debate: later gloss or Mosaic note. The verse displays the Canaanite: then in the land. The verse displays the Abram: a stranger. The note displays Abram a sojourner in a land held by others. The verse displays the Shechem: reached. The verse displays the Canaanite: present. The verse displays the promise: by faith. Abram's passing to Shechem and the note of the Canaanite display his sojourn and a crux: Abram came to Shechem, and the Canaanite was then in the land — the phrase read as a later gloss or post-Mosaic note (Clarke) or as a plain Mosaic observation that the land was already occupied (Ellicott); Abram a stranger in the land promised to his seed. The verse displays the sojourning Abram, to whom the LORD appears (v.7).
7:
"And the LORD appeared unto Abram, and said, Unto thy seed will I give this land: and there builded he an altar unto the LORD, who appeared unto him." The witnesses note the appearing of the LORD and the promise of the land to Abram's seed. On "the LORD appeared unto Abram," the grammatical voices note the theophany. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the LORD appeared unto Abram" — God appeared to Abram (the first recorded appearance of God to him in Canaan); the LORD confirmed his presence and promise by a visible manifestation; the theophany that sealed the covenant promise; God drawing near to his servant in the promised land.

On "Unto thy seed will I give this land," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the promise of the land. Calvin and Gill note that "Unto thy seed will I give this land" — God now names the land (the very land of Canaan, where Abram stood) and promises it to Abram's seed; the land-promise added to the seed-promise; though Abram himself would possess none of it (Acts 7:5), his seed (Israel, and ultimately the heavenly inheritance) would inherit the land; the covenant promise of the land confirmed.

On "there builded he an altar unto the LORD," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the altar: "there builded he an altar unto the LORD, who appeared unto him" — Abram built an altar to worship the God who appeared to him; the first of his altars in Canaan; the patriarch's worship, marking the place of God's appearing; Abram, a stranger in the land, set up the worship of the true God; the altar a witness of his faith and devotion. The verse displays the appearing of the LORD and the promise of the land.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appearing — "the LORD appeared unto Abram," the theophany confirming the promise (Keil, Ellicott); "Unto thy seed will I give this land," the land promised to Abram's seed (Acts 7:5, Calvin, Gill); "there builded he an altar," Abram's worship of the God who appeared (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appearing LORD. The verse marks the appearing: to Abram. The verse displays the promise: the land to his seed. The verse displays the altar: built. The verse displays the worship: of Abram. The theophany displays God confirming the promise and Abram worshiping. The verse displays the LORD: appeared. The verse displays the land: promised. The verse displays the altar: raised. The appearing of the LORD and the promise of the land display the confirmed covenant: the LORD appeared and promised the land to Abram's seed, and Abram built an altar — the theophany sealing the land-promise, and the patriarch's worship of the God who appeared. The verse displays the worshiping Abram, at Bethel (v.8).
8:
"And he removed from thence unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his tent, having Bethel on the west, and Hai on the east: and there he builded an altar unto the LORD, and called upon the name of the LORD." The witnesses note Abram's altar at Bethel and his calling on the name of the LORD. On "he removed from thence unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his tent," the grammatical voices note the journey. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he removed... unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his tent, having Bethel on the west, and Hai (Ai) on the east" — Abram journeyed on and pitched his tent between Bethel and Ai; the tent-dwelling of the pilgrim patriarch (Hebrews 11:9, dwelling in tents); the place named, where Abram would worship.

On "there he builded an altar unto the LORD," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the altar. Henry and Gill note that "there he builded an altar unto the LORD" — Abram built another altar (his second in Canaan), wherever he went setting up the worship of God; the patriarch's habit of worship; the altar marking his devotion in each place of his sojourn.

On "called upon the name of the LORD," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the worship: "called upon the name of the LORD" — Abram publicly worshiped and prayed, calling on the name of the LORD (invoking and proclaiming God); the public profession of the true religion amid the Canaanites; the patriarch's prayer and worship, a witness to the surrounding heathen; Abram, a stranger in the land, kept and proclaimed the worship of the true God. The verse displays Abram's altar at Bethel and his calling on the name of the LORD.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "he removed... unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his tent," Abram dwelling in tents between Bethel and Ai (Hebrews 11:9, Keil, Gill); "there he builded an altar unto the LORD," Abram's second altar, his habit of worship (Henry, Gill); "called upon the name of the LORD," Abram's public worship and profession amid the heathen (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshiping Abram. The verse marks the tent: pitched. The verse displays the altar: built. The verse displays the worship: calling on the LORD. The verse displays the profession: public. The altar displays Abram worshiping and proclaiming the true God. The verse displays the Abram: at Bethel. The verse displays the altar: raised. The verse displays the LORD: called upon. Abram's altar at Bethel and his calling on the name of the LORD display the patriarch's worship: he pitched his tent and built an altar between Bethel and Ai, and called on the name of the LORD — the pilgrim's tent-dwelling and his public worship of the true God amid the Canaanites. The verse displays the worshiping Abram, journeying south (v.9).
9:
"And Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south." The very brief verse notes Abram's continued journey toward the south. The grammatical voices note the movement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south" — Abram continued his journey, moving by stages toward the south (the Negev, the southern part of Canaan); the pilgrim patriarch traveling through the land; the gradual movement of Abram and his household through the country promised to his seed.

On "going on still toward the south," the witnesses note the pilgrimage. Gill notes that Abram went "still toward the south" — continuing his journey, not settling permanently, but moving as a pilgrim and stranger through the land; the tent-dwelling sojourner traversing Canaan; the patriarch's life of pilgrimage (Hebrews 11:9, dwelling in tents and journeying).

On the southward journey, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: Abram's journeying toward the south brought him toward Egypt (where the famine would drive him, v.10); the movement of the patriarch under God's providence; the pilgrim life of Abram, journeying through the land of promise without a settled possession (Acts 7:5); the stranger and sojourner moving on. The verse displays Abram's continued journey toward the south.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the movement — "Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south," Abram continuing toward the Negev (Keil, Gill); "going on still toward the south," the pilgrim patriarch traversing the land (Hebrews 11:9, Gill); the journey bringing him toward Egypt under providence (v.10, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying Abram. The verse marks the journey: continued. The verse displays the south: toward it. The verse displays the pilgrim: moving. The verse displays the providence: at work. The movement displays Abram traversing the land as a pilgrim. The verse displays the Abram: journeying. The verse displays the south: his way. The verse displays the pilgrimage: ongoing. Abram's continued journey toward the south displays the pilgrim patriarch: Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south — the tent-dwelling stranger traversing the land of promise without a settled possession (Acts 7:5), his journey bringing him toward Egypt. The verse displays the journeying Abram, driven by famine to Egypt (v.10).
10:
"And there was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there; for the famine was grievous in the land." The witnesses note the famine and Abram's going down into Egypt. On "there was a famine in the land," the grammatical voices note the trial. Ellicott and Gill note that "there was a famine in the land" — a famine struck the land of Canaan, the very land of promise; the trial of Abram's faith — scarcely arrived in the promised land, he meets famine; the testing of the patriarch, that the land flowing with promise should be visited with want; God's providence permitting the trial.

On "Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there," interpreters note the descent. The puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note that "Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there" — driven by the famine, Abram went down to Egypt (well-watered by the Nile, often a refuge in famine) to sojourn for a time; some commend his prudence in seeking relief; others note that he went without express direction from God, and that the descent into Egypt led him into temptation and sin (the deception over Sarai, vv.11-13); the trial that exposed Abram's weakness of faith.

On "the famine was grievous in the land," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the severity: "the famine was grievous in the land" — the famine was severe, pressing Abram to seek relief; the grievousness of the want drove him to Egypt; the hard providence that tested the patriarch's faith and brought him into danger. The verse displays the famine and Abram's going down into Egypt.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the famine — "there was a famine in the land," the trial of famine in the promised land (Ellicott, Gill); "Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there," Abram seeking refuge from the famine (whether prudently or without God's direction, Henry, Calvin); "the famine was grievous in the land," the severity that drove him (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the famine. The verse marks the famine: in the land. The verse displays the Egypt: gone down to. The verse displays the trial: of faith. The verse displays the descent: into danger. The famine displays Abram driven to Egypt by want. The verse displays the famine: grievous. The verse displays the Abram: gone down. The verse displays the trial: faced. The famine and Abram's going down into Egypt display the trial of faith in the promised land: a famine struck, and Abram went down to Egypt to sojourn — the patriarch, scarcely arrived in the land of promise, tried by want, going down to Egypt (whether prudently or without God's word) into temptation. The verse displays Abram in Egypt, fearing for his life (v.11).
11:
"And it came to pass, when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife, Behold now, I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon:" The witnesses note Abram's fear as he neared Egypt, because of Sarai's beauty. On "when he was come near to enter into Egypt," the grammatical voices note the occasion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife" — as Abram approached Egypt, he spoke to Sarai of his fear; the occasion of the deception (vv.12-13); the patriarch, anticipating danger in a strange and (he feared) lawless land, devised a plan.

On "Behold now, I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon," the reformed JFB and witnesses note Sarai's beauty. JFB and Gill note that Abram said, "I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon" — Sarai, though advanced in years (sixty-five or so), was still beautiful; her beauty was the occasion of Abram's fear, lest the Egyptians, taken with her, should kill him to take her; the cause of the danger Abram anticipated.

On Abram's fear, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the weakness: Abram's fear, looking to the danger and devising a scheme (vv.12-13), shows the weakness of his faith at this point — he who left all on God's promise now feared man and contrived a deception; the trial of Egypt exposed the patriarch's frailty; even the father of the faithful, in fear, faltered. The verse displays Abram's fear as he neared Egypt.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife," Abram speaking of his fear as he neared Egypt (Keil, Gill); "I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon," Sarai's beauty the cause of his fear (JFB, Gill); Abram's fear showing the weakness of his faith (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fearful Abram. The verse marks the Egypt: neared. The verse displays the Sarai: fair. The verse displays the fear: of Abram. The verse displays the weakness: of faith. The occasion displays Abram fearing danger because of Sarai's beauty. The verse displays the Abram: afraid. The verse displays the Sarai: beautiful. The verse displays the scheme: devised. Abram's fear as he neared Egypt displays the patriarch's frailty: nearing Egypt, Abram told Sarai he knew she was a fair woman — fearing the Egyptians would kill him for her beauty, the weakness of faith in the father of the faithful, who now feared man and devised a scheme. The verse displays the fearful Abram, anticipating danger (v.12).
12:
"Therefore it shall come to pass, when the Egyptians shall see thee, that they shall say, This is his wife: and they will kill me, but they will save thee alive." The brief verse notes Abram's fear that the Egyptians would kill him for Sarai. The grammatical voices note the anticipated danger. Gill and Calvin note that "when the Egyptians shall see thee, that they shall say, This is his wife: and they will kill me, but they will save thee alive" — Abram feared that the Egyptians, seeing Sarai's beauty and learning she was his wife, would kill him (the husband, the obstacle) and take her; his fear of the lawless violence he supposed in Egypt; the danger that prompted his deception (v.13).

On "they will kill me, but they will save thee alive," the reformed Calvin notes the fear. Calvin and Gill note that Abram anticipated being killed for his wife's sake; his fear of death drove him to the scheme of calling Sarai his sister (v.13); the weakness of faith that looked to the danger and trusted his own contrivance rather than God's protection; the patriarch, fearing man, sought to save his life by a half-truth.

On the anticipated danger, the grammatical-historical note the frailty: Abram's reasoning, while not groundless (such things did happen in the ancient world), led him into a course of deception that endangered Sarai's honor and dishonored his own faith; the fear of man brought a snare (Proverbs 29:25); the trial exposed Abram's weakness, that even the father of the faithful, in a strait, leaned on his own wit. The verse displays Abram's fear that the Egyptians would kill him.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the danger — "when the Egyptians shall see thee... they will kill me, but they will save thee alive," Abram fearing death for Sarai's beauty (Gill, Calvin); the fear of death driving the scheme (v.13, Calvin, Gill); the fear of man a snare, exposing Abram's weakness (Proverbs 29:25, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the anticipated danger. The verse marks the Egyptians: would see. The verse displays the killing: feared. The verse displays the Sarai: saved alive. The verse displays the scheme: prompted. The fear displays Abram dreading death for his wife's sake. The verse displays the Abram: fearing. The verse displays the death: anticipated. The verse displays the snare: the fear of man. Abram's fear that the Egyptians would kill him displays the snare of the fear of man: he feared they would kill him and take Sarai — the dread of death driving him to deception, the fear of man a snare (Proverbs 29:25), exposing the weakness of even the father of the faithful. The verse displays the fearful Abram, devising the half-truth (v.13).
13:
"Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister: that it may be well with me for thy sake; and my soul shall live because of thee." The witnesses note Abram's request that Sarai call herself his sister — the crux of the deception. On "Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister," interpreters divide over the act. The reformed Gill and many hold it a sin (or at least a fault): Abram, fearing for his life, put into Sarai's mouth a half-truth meant to deceive — though Sarai was indeed his half-sister (his sister by his father, 20:12), the intent was to conceal that she was his wife, and so to deceive; the deception sprang from unbelief and the fear of man; even the strongest believer is liable to sin, and Abram, famed for his faith, here fell into unbelief and into a fault that endangered his wife's honor. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and some note the mitigating truth: "my sister" was true literally, as Sarai was his half-sister; it was not a direct lie but an equivocation (a suppression of the whole truth); some judge Abram's act prudent self-preservation, others a culpable dissembling; the act is variously judged a sin, a fault, or an excusable (if unwise) stratagem.

On "that it may be well with me for thy sake; and my soul shall live because of thee," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the motive. Clarke and Gill note that Abram's motive was self-preservation — "that it may be well with me... and my soul shall live because of thee" — he sought to save his own life by the deception; the fear of death, not faith in God's protection, governed the scheme; the motive (however understandable) marks the weakness of his faith; he trusted his own wit rather than God to preserve him.

On the deception, the grammatical-historical note the consequence: the half-truth, far from securing safety, brought Sarai into Pharaoh's house and endangered the promise (vv.14-15); God had to intervene to protect Sarai and the covenant (v.17); the stratagem of unbelief brought trouble, and God's faithfulness, not Abram's wit, preserved them. The verse displays Abram's request that Sarai call herself his sister.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abram, fearing death, asked Sarai to call herself his sister, to save his life (Gill, Ellicott, Clarke). The act bears a crux: a sin/fault of unbelief and the fear of man — a deception endangering Sarai's honor (Gill) vs an equivocation literally true (she was his half-sister), variously judged a culpable dissembling or an excusable stratagem of self-preservation (Ellicott). The verse displays the deception crux. The verse marks the request: say thou art my sister. The verse displays the debate: sin, or excusable half-truth. The verse displays the motive: self-preservation. The verse displays the faith: weak. The request displays Abram's deception born of fear. The verse displays the Abram: dissembling. The verse displays the Sarai: his sister. The verse displays the unbelief: exposed. Abram's request that Sarai call herself his sister displays the deception and a crux: say thou art my sister, that it may be well with me — read as a sin of unbelief and the fear of man (Gill), or as an equivocation literally true, his half-sister, judged variously a fault or an excusable stratagem (Ellicott); the weakness of faith in the father of the faithful. The verse displays the dissembling Abram, his wife taken by Pharaoh (vv.14-15).
14:
"And it came to pass, that, when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair." The brief verse notes the Egyptians beholding Sarai's beauty. The grammatical voices note the fulfillment of Abram's fear. Gill and Henry note that "when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair" — on entering Egypt, the Egyptians saw and remarked Sarai's great beauty; the very thing Abram feared (v.11) came to pass; Sarai's beauty drew the attention of the Egyptians, and the deception (v.13) was set in motion.

On "the Egyptians beheld the woman," the witnesses note the attention. Gill notes that the Egyptians took notice of Sarai; her beauty (remarkable even in her age) was observed; the attention that would bring her to Pharaoh's notice (v.15); the danger Abram had anticipated.

On "that she was very fair," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the beauty: "very fair" — Sarai's beauty was exceptional; it occasioned the trouble that followed; the deception, meant to protect Abram, exposed Sarai to being taken; the consequence of beauty and of the half-truth; the trial unfolding as Abram feared, yet under God's overruling providence. The verse displays the Egyptians beholding Sarai's beauty.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the beholding — "when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair," the Egyptians remarking Sarai's beauty (Gill, Henry); the fear of Abram fulfilled (v.11, Gill); "that she was very fair," Sarai's exceptional beauty occasioning the trouble (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the beheld Sarai. The verse marks the Egyptians: beholding. The verse displays the Sarai: very fair. The verse displays the fear: fulfilled. The verse displays the danger: unfolding. The beholding displays Sarai's beauty drawing Egyptian attention. The verse displays the Sarai: observed. The verse displays the beauty: remarked. The verse displays the trouble: beginning. The Egyptians beholding Sarai's beauty display the unfolding of Abram's fear: the Egyptians saw that Sarai was very fair — her beauty drawing their attention, the danger Abram anticipated coming to pass, the deception set in motion. The verse displays the beheld Sarai, commended to Pharaoh (v.15).
15:
"The princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh: and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house." The witnesses note Sarai's being commended to Pharaoh and taken into his house. On "the princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh," the grammatical voices note the courtiers. Ellicott and Gill note that "the princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh" — Pharaoh's courtiers (princes, officials) saw Sarai and praised her beauty to the king; the attention rose from the people to the court; Sarai was commended to Pharaoh as a woman fit for his harem; the danger came to its height.

On "the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the taking. Calvin and Gill note that "the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house" — Sarai was taken into Pharaoh's house (his harem), with a view to becoming his wife; the deception (v.13), meant to protect Abram, now exposed Sarai to being taken by the king; the promise itself (the seed through Sarai) was endangered; the patriarch's stratagem had brought his wife into peril.

On the taking of Sarai, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the peril and providence: the taking of Sarai into Pharaoh's house was the grave consequence of the deception; yet God would intervene to protect her honor and the covenant promise (v.17); the providence of God overruling the danger that Abram's weak faith had brought; the LORD watching over Sarai and the promised seed. The verse displays Sarai's being taken into Pharaoh's house.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the commending — "the princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh," the courtiers praising Sarai to the king (Ellicott, Gill); "the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house," Sarai taken into the harem, the promise endangered (Calvin, Gill); God's intervention to protect her (v.17, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the taken Sarai. The verse marks the princes: commending. The verse displays the Pharaoh: the king. The verse displays the Sarai: taken. The verse displays the promise: endangered. The taking displays Sarai brought into Pharaoh's house. The verse displays the Sarai: in peril. The verse displays the deception: its fruit. The verse displays the providence: to intervene. Sarai's being commended to Pharaoh and taken into his house display the grave consequence of the deception: the princes commended her, and Sarai was taken into Pharaoh's house — the stratagem exposing Sarai to the king and endangering the promise, awaiting God's intervention to protect her. The verse displays Sarai in peril, with Abram enriched (v.16).
16:
"And he entreated Abram well for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen, and he asses, and menservants, and maidservants, and she asses, and camels." The brief verse notes Abram's enrichment by Pharaoh for Sarai's sake. The grammatical voices note the gifts. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he entreated Abram well for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen, and he asses, and menservants, and maidservants, and she asses, and camels" — Pharaoh treated Abram well for Sarai's sake (supposing him her brother), enriching him with flocks, herds, servants, and camels; Abram gained great wealth through the deception; the gifts of the king, given on a false supposition.

On "he entreated Abram well for her sake," the witnesses note the favor. Gill notes that Pharaoh, taking Sarai as his intended wife, honored her supposed brother Abram with gifts; the favor shown to Abram was the fruit of the deception; the patriarch enriched, but at the cost of his wife's peril and his own integrity.

On the gifts, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the irony: the wealth Abram gained — flocks, herds, servants, camels — came through the very deception that endangered Sarai and the promise; the gifts were a doubtful gain, won by a fault; the riches of the patriarch, increased here, would later be the occasion of the strife with Lot (13:6-7); the providence of God permitting Abram to prosper even through his fault, yet the gain was tainted. The verse displays Abram's enrichment by Pharaoh for Sarai's sake.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the gifts — "he entreated Abram well for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen... and camels," Pharaoh enriching Abram for Sarai's sake (Keil, Gill); the favor the fruit of the deception (Gill); the wealth a doubtful gain, won by a fault (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enriched Abram. The verse marks the Pharaoh: entreating well. The verse displays the gifts: flocks, servants, camels. The verse displays the Sarai: for her sake. The verse displays the wealth: gained. The enrichment displays Abram prospered through the deception. The verse displays the Abram: enriched. The verse displays the gifts: many. The verse displays the gain: doubtful. Abram's enrichment by Pharaoh for Sarai's sake displays the doubtful gain of the deception: Pharaoh entreated Abram well and gave him flocks, herds, servants, and camels — the patriarch enriched on a false supposition, the wealth won by a fault that endangered Sarai and the promise. The verse displays the enriched Abram, before God's plague on Pharaoh (v.17).
17:
"And the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai Abram's wife." The witnesses note the LORD's plaguing of Pharaoh to protect Sarai. On "the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues," the grammatical voices note the divine intervention. Gill and Calvin note that "the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues" — God intervened, sending plagues (afflictions, perhaps diseases) upon Pharaoh and his household; the divine protection of Sarai and the covenant promise; God, not Abram's wit, preserved Sarai's honor and the line of the promised seed; the LORD watching over his own.

On "because of Sarai Abram's wife," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the cause. Calvin and Gill note that the plagues came "because of Sarai Abram's wife" — God plagued Pharaoh on account of Sarai, to prevent her being defiled and to deliver her; God's regard to the covenant and to Sarai (the mother of the promised seed); the plagues forced Pharaoh to discover the truth and to restore Sarai to Abram (vv.18-19); God's faithful protection of the promise, despite Abram's fault.

On the plagues, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: God's intervention shows that the safety of Sarai and the promise rested not on Abram's stratagem but on God's faithfulness; the LORD overruled the danger that Abram's weak faith had brought; the deliverance of Sarai was God's work; the providence of God preserving the covenant line. The verse displays the LORD's plaguing of Pharaoh to protect Sarai.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the intervention — "the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues," God sending plagues to protect Sarai (Gill, Calvin); "because of Sarai Abram's wife," the plagues for Sarai's sake, to deliver her and guard the promise (Calvin, Gill); God's faithfulness preserving the covenant despite Abram's fault (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the plaguing LORD. The verse marks the plagues: great. The verse displays the Pharaoh: plagued. The verse displays the Sarai: protected. The verse displays the promise: guarded. The intervention displays God preserving Sarai and the covenant. The verse displays the LORD: plaguing. The verse displays the Sarai: delivered. The verse displays the providence: faithful. The LORD's plaguing of Pharaoh to protect Sarai displays God's faithful protection of the promise: the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house because of Sarai — God's intervention preserving Sarai's honor and the covenant line, the deliverance resting on God's faithfulness, not Abram's stratagem. The verse displays the plagued Pharaoh, rebuking Abram (vv.18-19).
18:
"And Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me? why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?" The witnesses note Pharaoh's rebuke of Abram for the deception. On "Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me?" the grammatical voices note the rebuke. Gill and Calvin note that "Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me?" — Pharaoh, learning the truth (by the plagues, v.17), summoned and rebuked Abram; the heathen king reproved the patriarch for the deception that had brought trouble on his house; the humbling of Abram, rebuked by a pagan for his fault.

On "why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the reproach. Calvin and Gill note that Pharaoh reproached Abram — "why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?" — for concealing the truth and exposing him to the danger of taking another man's wife; the justice of the rebuke; the patriarch, who should have been a witness of the truth, was reproved by a heathen for his deception; the shame of Abram's fault.

On Pharaoh's rebuke, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the lesson: the rebuke of the believer by an unbeliever is a humbling thing; Abram's deception, meant to save himself, dishonored his faith and his God before the heathen; the fault of God's servant brought reproach; yet God's faithfulness delivered him, and the rebuke was deserved; a warning against the fear of man and the resort to deception. The verse displays Pharaoh's rebuke of Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rebuke — "Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me?" Pharaoh reproving Abram for the deception (Gill, Calvin); "why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?" the just reproach for concealing the truth (Calvin, Gill); the humbling of the believer rebuked by a heathen (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rebuked Abram. The verse marks the Pharaoh: rebuking. The verse displays the deed: what hast thou done. The verse displays the wife: not told. The verse displays the shame: of Abram. The rebuke displays Pharaoh reproving the patriarch's fault. The verse displays the Pharaoh: reproving. The verse displays the Abram: humbled. The verse displays the deception: exposed. Pharaoh's rebuke of Abram displays the humbling of the believer for his fault: Pharaoh asked, What is this thou hast done? why didst thou not tell me she was thy wife? — the heathen king justly reproving the patriarch for the deception, the shame of Abram's fault before the world. The verse displays the rebuked Abram, sent away (vv.19-20).
19:
"Why saidst thou, She is my sister? so I might have taken her to me to wife: now therefore behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way." The witnesses note Pharaoh restoring Sarai and dismissing Abram. On "Why saidst thou, She is my sister? so I might have taken her to me to wife," the grammatical voices note the reproach. Ellicott and Gill note that "Why saidst thou, She is my sister? so I might have taken her to me to wife" — Pharaoh reproached Abram for the half-truth that had nearly led him to take Sarai as his wife; the deception had brought Pharaoh to the brink of a great wrong (unwittingly taking another's wife); the king's just complaint at the danger Abram's words had caused.

On "now therefore behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the restoration. Calvin and Gill note that Pharaoh restored Sarai to Abram — "behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way" — the king, having learned the truth and feeling the plagues (v.17), returned Sarai untouched and bade Abram depart; the deliverance of Sarai, by God's overruling providence; the promise preserved, Sarai restored to her husband.

On the dismissal, the grammatical-historical note the providence: Pharaoh's restoring of Sarai and his dismissal of Abram show God's hand in delivering Sarai and the covenant; the very king who took her now gladly sent her away; God's faithfulness, working even through the heathen king, preserved the promise; Abram, rebuked but delivered, departed with his wife. The verse displays Pharaoh restoring Sarai and dismissing Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reproach — "Why saidst thou, She is my sister? so I might have taken her to me to wife," Pharaoh's just complaint at the danger (Ellicott, Gill); "behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way," Sarai restored to Abram (Calvin, Gill); God's providence delivering Sarai and the promise (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the restored Sarai. The verse marks the reproach: Pharaoh's. The verse displays the wife: restored. The verse displays the Abram: dismissed. The verse displays the promise: preserved. The restoration displays Sarai returned to Abram by God's providence. The verse displays the Sarai: given back. The verse displays the Abram: sent away. The verse displays the providence: faithful. Pharaoh restoring Sarai and dismissing Abram displays the deliverance of the promise: behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way — Pharaoh, rebuking the deception, restored Sarai untouched and sent Abram away, God's providence delivering Sarai and preserving the covenant line. The verse displays the restored Sarai, with Abram's departure (v.20).
20:
"And Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him: and they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had." The witnesses note Abram's being sent away from Egypt with his household. On "Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him," the grammatical voices note the escort. Gill and Clarke note that "Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him" — Pharaoh gave his men orders concerning Abram, to conduct him safely out of Egypt; the king, anxious to be rid of the cause of the plagues, arranged for Abram's departure; the patriarch escorted out, with his wife and goods, under Pharaoh's protection.

On "they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the departure. Calvin and Gill note that "they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had" — Abram was sent away with Sarai restored and all his possessions (including the wealth gained, v.16); the patriarch departed unharmed, with his household and goods; God's providence brought him safely out of Egypt, the promise preserved, despite his fault.

On the sending away, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: Abram left Egypt richer in goods but humbled by his fault; the deliverance from Egypt, by God's mercy, restored him to the land of promise (13:1); the providence of God, faithful through Abram's weakness, brought him out with his wife and substance; the patriarch returned to Canaan, to renew his pilgrimage and worship. The verse displays Abram's being sent away from Egypt.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the escort — "Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him," Pharaoh arranging Abram's safe departure (Gill, Clarke); "they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had," Abram departing with Sarai and his goods (Calvin, Gill); God's providence bringing him safely out (13:1, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departing Abram. The verse marks the Pharaoh: commanding. The verse displays the Abram: sent away. The verse displays the wife: restored. The verse displays the goods: kept. The departure displays Abram brought safely out of Egypt. The verse displays the Abram: escorted. The verse displays the household: with him. The verse displays the providence: faithful. Abram's being sent away from Egypt with his household displays the deliverance of the patriarch: Pharaoh's men sent Abram away with his wife and all he had — the patriarch brought safely out of Egypt by God's providence, the promise preserved despite his fault, to return to the land of promise. The verse closes the Egypt episode, before Abram's return (ch.13).
